draft.introduction: Book III of De Rerum Natura argues that the soul is mortal and perishes with the body. Lucretius marshals over thirty arguments against the soul’s survival of death, aiming to free humanity from the fear of an afterlife.

translation.original_ref: Lucretius, De Rerum Natura III.830–842 (Bailey, OCT)
translation.text: Death is nothing to us and concerns us not at all, since the nature of the mind is understood to be mortal. And just as in time gone by we felt no distress when the armies of Carthage pressed on every side, so when we shall no longer exist, when the parting of body and soul from which we are made shall have come about, nothing at all will be able to happen to us who then will no longer be.
translation.original_text: Nil igitur mors est ad nos neque pertinet hilum, quandoquidem natura animi mortalis habetur.

translator_notes.note: The “symmetry argument”—that postmortem nonexistence is no worse than prenatal nonexistence—is Lucretius’s most celebrated philosophical move. It was later debated by modern philosophers under the name “the Lucretian asymmetry problem.”
